gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — bitter gourd / bitter melon analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this composite dish. GI estimated at 25 (low) based on the dominant bitter gourd component, which is a very low-carbohydrate vegetable with negligible glycaemic impact. Pork floss contributes protein and fat with no meaningful carbohydrate load. Carbohydrate per 100g is reported as 0.0g by HPB; therefore glycemic load per 150g serving is 0.0. Analogous reference: bitter melon / bitter gourd classified as low-GI vegetable in University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Leung et al., 2009, British Journal of Nutrition).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1050 mg/100g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100g upper threshold for hypertension-safe foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish; sodium at 1050 mg/100g is driven by pork floss (a high-sodium processed dried meat) and fish sauce, and a 150 g serving would deliver approximately 1575 mg sodium — exceeding the entire recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Pork floss is a processed meat with added sugar and high sodium that offsets the hypoglycaemic benefit of bitter gourd
type2_diabetes.guidance: While bitter gourd contains charantin and polypeptide-p with evidence-based hypoglycaemic properties, the pork floss component introduces added sugar, high sodium, and processed-meat insulin-resistance risk; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should request preparation with minimal pork floss, reduced fish sauce, and no added sugar to preserve the beneficial bitter gourd effect.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (1050 mg/100g), cholesterol (89 mg/100g), and processed pork floss content elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of very high sodium (1050 mg/100g), dietary cholesterol (89 mg/100g), and processed pork floss — a product associated with elevated LDL and arterial inflammation — makes this dish unsuitable for individuals with cardiovascular disease without significant modification; bitter gourd itself is cardioprotective but is insufficient to offset the sodium and processed-meat burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork floss is pro-inflammatory due to high sodium and arachidonic acid content; sodium at 1050 mg/100g is a hard disqualifier for RA
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish as the pork floss component is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6) and sodium, both of which amplify inflammatory cytokine production and worsen joint swelling; substituting pork floss with tofu floss and eliminating fish sauce would substantially reduce the inflammatory burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bitter Gourd with Pork Floss (Kho Qua Cha Bong) is a Vietnamese-influenced dish popular in Singapore, consisting of stir-fried or blanched bitter gourd (Momordica charantia) topped or mixed with pork floss (cha bong), a dry, fluffy, fibrous processed pork product made by slow-cooking and shredding seasoned pork. The dish is seasoned with fish sauce and other condiments, giving it a savoury, slightly bitter, and umami-rich flavour profile. Bitter gourd contributes a distinctive sharp bitterness and crisp-tender texture, while pork floss adds a light, airy, mildly sweet-savoury contrast. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (19.1 g/100g) and moderate in fat (10.5 g/100g), with negligible carbohydrate (0.0 g/100g) as reported by HPB Singapore. Sodium is very high at 1050 mg/100g, driven by pork floss and fish sauce, making the dish a concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. Bitter gourd contains bioactive compounds including charantin, polypeptide-p, and vicine, which have evidence-based hypoglycaemic properties relevant to Type 2 Diabetes management, though these benefits are partially offset by the processed pork floss component. The dish is commonly served as a side dish at lunch or dinner in Singaporean hawker centres and Vietnamese-Singaporean households. It is not halal due to the pork content. The glycemic load per serving is effectively zero given the negligible carbohydrate content.
